summary: TypeInitializationException for SKData when deploying a .NET 6 server-side app to Docker on GCP — caused by missing or conflicting SkiaSharp NativeAssets.Linux packages in the container, not a code bug.

stepsToReproduce: Create a .NET 6 app using SkiaSharp to resize images
Install SkiaSharp 2.88.5 along with NativeAssets.Linux, NativeAssets.Linux.NoDependencies, NativeAssets.WebAssembly, and Views.Blazor
Deploy to GCP Container Registry with Docker
Call any SkiaSharp API (e.g. SKImage.FromEncodedData) — TypeInitializationException is thrown
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: .NET 6, SkiaSharp 2.88.5, Docker on GCP Linux, Visual Studio 2022

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The underlying cause is a packaging/deployment issue, not a version-specific bug. The same problem can occur with any SkiaSharp version if the native library is not properly deployed in the container.

analysis.summary: The TypeInitializationException occurs because SKData's static constructor calls SkiaApi.sk_data_new_empty() via P/Invoke, which requires libSkiaSharp.so to be loaded. In the Docker container, the native library either isn't present or the wrong variant is loaded. The reporter installed conflicting packages (both NativeAssets.Linux and NoDependencies, plus unnecessary WASM and Blazor packages), which likely causes deployment confusion.
analysis.rationale: This is a deployment/packaging question, not a code bug. The reporter's code works locally on Windows. The exception is a standard DllNotFoundException wrapped in TypeInitializationException — the native library isn't being found in the Docker container. The reporter's package list shows a misunderstanding of which packages are needed. Comments confirm that using NoDependencies (with or without fontconfig/freetype) resolves the issue.

keySignals.text: The type initializer for 'SkiaSharp.SKData' threw an exception
keySignals.interpretation: SKData's static constructor fails because libSkiaSharp.so cannot be loaded via P/Invoke — classic DllNotFoundException wrapped in TypeInitializationException.
Reporter installed conflicting/unnecessary packages. Having both NativeAssets.Linux and NoDependencies can cause the wrong binary to be deployed.

keySignals.text: I am not using Blazor or Web assembly specifically
keySignals.interpretation: Confirms this is a server-side Linux app in Docker, not WASM. Views.Blazor and NativeAssets.WebAssembly are unnecessary.

keySignals.text: This worked for me after adding missing 2 libs
keySignals.interpretation: Community-confirmed workaround: NoDependencies + libfontconfig1 + libfreetype6 in Dockerfile.

codeInvestigation.finding: SKData's static constructor calls SkiaApi.sk_data_new_empty() which P/Invokes into libSkiaSharp. If the native library isn't loaded, this throws DllNotFoundException which is wrapped in TypeInitializationException.
SKObject.EnsureStaticInstanceAreInitialized() triggers SKData's static constructor early, meaning ANY SkiaSharp API call will trigger the exception if the native library is missing.

Documentation clearly states: Docker containers should use NativeAssets.Linux.NoDependencies for zero system deps, or NativeAssets.Linux with fontconfig installed. Reporter had both packages which can cause conflicts.

workarounds: Remove all unnecessary packages (Views.Blazor, NativeAssets.WebAssembly). Use ONLY SkiaSharp + SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux.NoDependencies for Docker containers.
If using NativeAssets.Linux instead of NoDependencies, add 'RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libfontconfig1' to Dockerfile.
Ensure the NativeAssets package is referenced in the executable project, not just a library project.

resolution.hypothesis: The native library libSkiaSharp.so is not present or the wrong variant is loaded in the Docker container because the reporter installed conflicting NativeAssets packages.

proposals.title: Use NoDependencies only
proposals.description: Remove all NativeAssets packages except SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux.NoDependencies. Remove SkiaSharp.Views.Blazor and SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.WebAssembly as they are not needed for a server-side app. The NoDependencies package has zero external dependencies and works in minimal Docker containers.

proposals.title: Use NativeAssets.Linux with fontconfig
proposals.description: If system font enumeration is needed, use SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux (not NoDependencies) and install fontconfig in the Dockerfile: 'RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libfontconfig1'.

resolution.recommendedReason: Simplest approach for Docker containers with no system library dependencies. Community-confirmed in comments on this issue.

actionability.reason: This is a packaging/deployment question with a well-documented solution. The community has already confirmed the workaround in the issue comments, and documentation/packages.md covers this scenario.

actions.description: Reclassify as question about native asset deployment on Linux
Post summary with correct package guidance
actions.comment:
Thanks for the report. This is a native library deployment issue — the `TypeInitializationException` for `SKData` means `libSkiaSharp.so` could not be loaded in your Docker container.

The issue is that you have conflicting packages installed. For a server-side .NET app in Docker, you only need two packages:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="SkiaSharp" Version="2.88.5" />
<PackageReference Include="SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux.NoDependencies" Version="2.88.5" />
```

Remove `SkiaSharp.Views.Blazor`, `SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.WebAssembly`, and `SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux` — these are for different platforms and having them all can cause the wrong native binary to be deployed.

If you need system font enumeration (via `SKFontManager`), use `SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux` instead and add `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libfontconfig1` to your Dockerfile.

The NativeAssets package must be in the **application** (executable) project, not a library project, to ensure the native binary is included in the publish output.

actions.description: Cross-reference the original Blazor WASM issue the reporter was following
Close as answered — community-confirmed workaround exists
